qa_pairs.question: When will `StdEnv/2023` become the default environment for all Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: `StdEnv/2023` will become the default environment for all Alliance clusters after April 1, 2024.

qa_pairs.question: How can a user specify their own default standard environment on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: A user can specify their own default standard environment by modifying the `$HOME/.modulerc` file.

qa_pairs.question: What command would set `StdEnv/2020` as the default environment for a user on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: The command `echo "module-version StdEnv/2020 default" >> $HOME/.modulerc` will set `StdEnv/2020` as the default environment.

qa_pairs.question: What action is required for a change to the default standard environment to take effect on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: The user must log out and log in again for the change to the default standard environment to take effect.

qa_pairs.question: Is it necessary to reinstall or recompile code when the `StdEnv` changes on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, if you compile your own code or have installed R or Python packages, you should recompile your code or reinstall the packages with the newest version of the standard environment.

qa_pairs.question: How can a user continue to use an earlier standard environment for an existing workflow on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: To use an earlier environment, a user should add `module load StdEnv/2020` to their job scripts before loading any other modules.

qa_pairs.question: Will earlier standard environments be removed from Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: No, earlier environments and software dependent on them will remain available, but versions 2016.4 and 2018.3 are no longer supported.

qa_pairs.question: Which older standard environments are no longer supported on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: Versions 2016.4 and 2018.3 of the standard environments are no longer supported.

qa_pairs.question: Does Alliance staff install software in older standard environments?
qa_pairs.answer: No, Alliance staff will only install software in the new `StdEnv/2023` environment.

qa_pairs.question: Can modules from different standard environments be mixed within a single job on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: No, you should use a single standard environment for a given job, as mixing them can lead to unpredictable results and errors.

qa_pairs.question: Which standard environment should a user employ for a new project or to use a newer version of an application on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: For a new project or to use a newer version of an application, users should use `StdEnv/2023` by adding `module load StdEnv/2023` to their job scripts.

qa_pairs.question: Is loading modules in a user's `.bashrc` file recommended on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: No, loading modules in your `.bashrc` is not recommended; instead, modules should be explicitly loaded in job scripts.

qa_pairs.question: Do the `StdEnv` changes on Alliance clusters affect users who only use cloud resources?
qa_pairs.answer: No, these changes only affect available software accessed by using environment modules on HPC clusters, not cloud resources.

qa_pairs.question: What should a user do if they can no longer load a module they previously used on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should run the `module avail` command to see available versions of applications in the new environment, as more recent versions are typically installed there.

qa_pairs.question: How can a user check the available versions of an application like GCC within the new standard environment on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: A user can run the command `module avail gcc` to see the available versions of GCC compilers.
